_note: Per-problem clinical triage. Authored strictly from each root cause's existing differentiators plus standard, well-established red flags. Weights point answers at root_cause ids that must exist on the problem. Expert-extendable; parse.js folds this onto graph.problems[].assessment.

knee-pain.intro: Four quick taps — this points you to the most likely cause, the way a physiotherapist would triage it.

options.label: Behind or around the kneecap
Right below the kneecap
Deep, or across the whole joint
Going down stairs, squatting, or after sitting a while
Jumping, running, or explosive loading
Stiffness first thing in the morning or after resting

questions.q: How did it come on, and your age?

options.label: Gradually, from training or overuse — I'm under 40
Slowly over months, and I'm 40 or older

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these?
redFlags.sub: Tap all that apply. These are the signs that need an in-person exam, not a self-care plan.
flags: The knee locks, catches, or suddenly gives way
I can't put weight on it, or can't fully straighten it
It's hot, red, or swollen — or the pain wakes me at night
It started right after a significant injury, fall, or twist
redFlags.message: What you've described — locking or giving way, being unable to bear weight, a hot or swollen joint, night pain, or a recent significant injury — should be checked in person before you start any self-care plan. Please see a doctor or physiotherapist first. You can still read the protocol below for background, but get assessed.

knee-pain.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

low-back-pain.intro: A few quick taps — this points to whether your back mostly needs strengthening or better control, the way a physio would triage it.

questions.q: When does it bother you most?

options.label: A dull ache after sitting or standing a while, easing once I move
Sudden 'tweaks' or catches with quick or heavy movements
A fairly constant low-grade ache and stiffness
Fine most of the time, then it grabs unpredictably
Mostly sedentary / deconditioned
Active, but my back gives way on certain movements

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these?
redFlags.sub: Tap all that apply. These need an in-person exam, not a self-care plan.
flags: Pain, numbness or tingling running down into the leg or foot
New weakness in a leg, or trouble controlling your bladder or bowels
Numbness around the groin or saddle area
Pain after a significant fall or accident, or with unexplained weight loss or fever
Night pain that wakes you and isn't eased by changing position
redFlags.message: What you've described can signal nerve involvement or, rarely, an emergency (cauda equina) — please get assessed in person before any self-care plan. You can read the protocol below for background, but see a doctor or physiotherapist first.

low-back-pain.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

neck-shoulder.intro: A few quick taps — this points to whether it's posture-driven neck tension or a shoulder-joint problem.

options.label: Tight neck and upper traps, rounded shoulders, tension headaches
Pain in the shoulder itself when I lift my arm
Long hours at a desk or on my phone
Reaching overhead — and it aches at night on that side

questions.q: Is there a painful arc lifting your arm?

options.label: No real arc — more general tension and stiffness
Yes — a painful band partway up when I raise my arm

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these?
flags: Arm weakness, numbness, or pins and needles down the arm or into the hand
Pain after a fall or injury, or you can't lift the arm at all
The shoulder looks deformed or out of place
Neck pain after trauma, or with fever or unexplained weight loss
redFlags.message: These signs can point to nerve, fracture or other problems that need an in-person exam — please see a doctor or physiotherapist before starting a self-care plan.

neck-shoulder.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

insulin-resistance.intro: A few quick taps — this points to whether inactivity or belly fat is the bigger driver of your blood-sugar issue.

options.label: Energy crashes after meals; I'm just not very active
A growing belly/waist that seems to be pushing my blood sugar up
Not especially large — it's more about inactivity
Larger waist / central weight gain
Lean-ish but sedentary
Carrying extra weight around the middle

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these?
redFlags.sub: Tap all that apply. These need proper testing, not self-management.
flags: Very frequent urination and thirst, blurred vision, or unexplained weight loss
A blood-sugar reading in the diabetic range, or you already have diabetes
Numbness or tingling in the feet
redFlags.message: These can signal diabetes that needs proper testing (HbA1c / fasting glucose) and medical care — please see a doctor before self-managing. The protocol below is background only.

insulin-resistance.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

visceral-fat.intro: A few quick taps — this points to whether a plain energy surplus or a stress/cortisol pattern is driving the belly fat.

options.label: Gradual — I'm honestly eating more than I burn
Stress-driven — poor sleep, stress eating, gain even when I 'eat okay'
Both fine — it's mainly diet and activity
Poor sleep and high stress
Fairly general weight gain
Mostly belly, with fatigue and cravings

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these?
flags: Rapid belly-fat gain with purple stretch marks, easy bruising, or a rounding face
Very high blood pressure or blood sugar alongside the weight gain
redFlags.message: Rapid central weight gain with those features can, rarely, signal a hormonal condition — worth a doctor's check before self-managing.

visceral-fat.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

chronic-fatigue.intro: A few quick taps — this points to whether low iron or a deeper mitochondrial pattern is behind the fatigue.

options.label: Tired and breathless on exertion, looking pale
Low energy even though I sleep well and my iron is normal
Heavy periods, a vegetarian/vegan diet, or a recent blood donation
None of those apply to me

questions.q: Breathless or heart racing on stairs?

options.label: Yes, and I look pale
No — just a flat, steady low energy

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these?
redFlags.sub: Tap all that apply. Fatigue can have serious causes worth ruling out.
flags: Unexplained weight loss, drenching night sweats, or fever
Breathless at rest, chest pain, or a very fast or irregular heartbeat
Very heavy bleeding, or blood in your stool
Fatigue that is rapidly getting worse over days to weeks
redFlags.message: Please get a ferritin + full blood count and see a doctor before self-treating — these signs need proper assessment first. The protocol below is background only.

chronic-fatigue.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

low-testosterone.intro: A few quick taps — this points to whether lifestyle or a confirmed clinical cause is the better starting point.

options.label: Not yet — I just have symptoms (low drive, fatigue, poor recovery)
Yes — repeat morning tests confirmed low testosterone
Overweight, poor sleep, high stress, low activity
Dialled in — yet levels are still low
Fix the basics naturally first
Explore medical management

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these?
flags: Very low libido with headaches or vision changes
New breast tissue development, or infertility with loss of morning erections
Testicular pain, a lump, or a history of undescended testes
redFlags.message: These can point to a medical cause that needs a doctor (hormone panel, sometimes imaging) — please get assessed rather than self-treating.

low-testosterone.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

skin-aging.intro: A few quick taps — this points to whether to prioritise rebuilding collagen or repairing sun/UV damage.

options.label: Fine lines, loss of firmness and glow — overall ageing
Sun spots, uneven tone, patchy pigmentation
General ageing, plus a sweet tooth and time
Lots of past sun exposure or tanning
Crepey and less plump
Dark spots and blotchy tone

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these on your skin?
redFlags.sub: Tap all that apply. These should be checked before any cosmetic routine.
flags: A mole or spot changing in size, shape or colour, or that bleeds
A sore that won't heal, or a new firm bump that's growing
A patch with irregular borders or several colours
redFlags.message: Any changing, bleeding or non-healing spot should be checked by a doctor or dermatologist to rule out skin cancer before starting a cosmetic routine.

skin-aging.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

hair-loss.intro: A few quick taps — this points to whether it's pattern (DHT) loss or shedding from a nutrient shortfall.

options.label: A receding hairline or thinning crown; it runs in my family
Diffuse shedding all over, after dieting, stress or with low iron
Slow, gradual thinning over years, in a pattern
Noticeable shedding that started after a trigger (crash diet, illness, stress)
Family history of male/female pattern loss
Low iron/ferritin, low protein, or a restrictive diet

redFlags.q: Before we go on — any of these?
flags: Patchy bald spots, or hair coming out in clumps
Redness, scaling, scarring or pain of the scalp
Sudden loss alongside weight change, fatigue or feeling the cold (possible thyroid issue)
redFlags.message: Patchy loss, a scarring or inflamed scalp, or loss with other symptoms should be seen by a doctor or dermatologist — some causes are treatable and best caught early.

hair-loss.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.

longevity.intro: A few quick taps — this points to the longevity approach that fits you best. Both are valid; it's about your starting angle.

options.label: Clearing 'zombie' senescent cells and boosting mitochondria
Cycling growth vs repair — fasting-style autophagy
General anti-ageing and healthspan
I tend to over-eat / stay in surplus and want more repair
Senolytics, NAD⁺, mitochondrial support
Fasting, rapamycin, caloric restriction

redFlags.q: Before we go on — do any of these apply?
redFlags.sub: Tap all that apply. If so, aggressive fasting or longevity compounds may not be safe for you.
flags: You're underweight, or have a history of disordered eating
You're pregnant or breastfeeding
You take medication for diabetes or blood pressure (fasting can interact)
redFlags.message: If any of these apply, please check with a doctor before aggressive fasting or longevity compounds — the plan below can be read for background.

longevity.notMedicalAdvice: This is an educational self-check, not a diagnosis. If in doubt, see a clinician.
